frameworks.name: First Impression Anchoring Framework
frameworks.definition: Initial decisions and impressions on a stimulus (like checking into a hotel) anchor all subsequent perceptions of that same stimulus, with evidence-seeking behavior reinforcing the initial judgment.

claims.text: Charlie Munger (via Warren Buffett's partnership) describes the human brain as functioning like an egg: once an initial decision, first impression, or prejudice enters the brain, it closes down to further stimuli and prevents contradictory information from changing the initial perception.
claims.speaker: Charlie Munger
claims.mechanism: The brain commits to an initial decision and then selectively seeks affirmatory evidence while dismissing contradictory perceptions, creating a closed feedback loop.

claims.text: Hotel guests who experienced a good check-in within three minutes rated all subsequent aspects of the hotel (service, amenities, even subjective features like ice sculptures) as good, because they made an initial decision that the hotel was good and then looked for affirmatory evidence to confirm it.
claims.speaker: Charlie Munger
conditions: check-in experience within first three minutes
claims.mechanism: First impression anchors subsequent perception through selective evidence-seeking behavior.

claims.text: Hotel guests who experienced a 15-20 minute wait for check-in rated everything about the hotel negatively, including subjective features unrelated to service (the ice sculpture in the garden), demonstrating that initial negative impressions reframe all subsequent perceptions.
claims.speaker: Charlie Munger
claims.mechanism: Negative first impression triggers systematic reframing of all subsequent observations through a negative filter.
bounded_by: Limited to observable satisfaction questionnaire responses from hotel guests

claims.text: Guests who queued for 20 minutes at check-in were measurably eager to express negative satisfaction with unrelated hotel features (ice sculpture) on satisfaction questionnaires, indicating that initial emotional negative experiences contaminate all subsequent judgments.
claims.speaker: Charlie Munger

anchors.value: negative satisfaction ratings on unrelated features

bounded_by: Observed only in questionnaire responses; does not measure actual service quality or feature properties

examples.text: A hotel guest checks in and waits three minutes, experiencing efficient service and positive interaction. They then tour the hotel, evaluate the ice sculpture in the garden, eat at the restaurant, and sleep in the room. Their initial positive impression anchors their perception: they notice and remember positive aspects, overlook minor inconveniences, and rate the ice sculpture favorably on their departure survey because their decision to like the hotel is locked in.

A different hotel guest queues for 20 minutes at check-in, growing frustrated and forming a negative impression. They then tour the same hotel with identical physical features, but their initial negative decision reframes everything: the same ice sculpture now looks tacky, the restaurant service feels slow, the room feels small. On their departure survey, they actively mark negative ratings on features—even ones they did not directly experience—because their brain is locked into the negative decision.

practices.text: In service-oriented contexts, optimize the first customer touchpoint (check-in, onboarding, initial call) to deliver a positive experience, as this initial decision will anchor all downstream perceptions and satisfaction.

Avoid creating friction in early customer interactions, as negative first impressions trigger systematic reframing of all subsequent experiences, including unrelated aspects of the service or product.
